questions.question: What is the minimum period of recurrent abdominal pain to diagnose IBS according to Rome IV?
questions.explanation: The Rome IV criteria require recurrent abdominal pain at least one day per week for the past 3 months, with onset of symptoms at least 6 months before diagnosis.

questions.question: Which of the following characteristics must accompany abdominal pain to meet Rome IV criteria?
questions.explanation: The Rome IV criteria require that abdominal pain be associated with two or more of: relationship to defecation, change in stool frequency, or change in stool form.

questions.question: With what minimum weekly frequency should abdominal pain occur in Rome IV?
questions.explanation: Rome IV states that abdominal pain must occur at least one day per week for the past 3 months to meet the frequency criterion.

questions.question: Which of the following symptoms is a supporting Rome IV criterion for IBS?
questions.explanation: Abdominal distension is a supporting symptom in Rome IV, although it is not mandatory for the diagnosis, and helps differentiate subtypes of IBS.

questions.question: What is the minimum total symptom time required before diagnosis of IBS according to Rome IV?
questions.explanation: Rome IV requires that symptoms have started at least 6 months before diagnosis, although recurrent pain is assessed within the last 3 months.

questions.question: What change in depositions is considered a supporting criterion in Rome IV?
questions.explanation: Rome IV includes as a supporting criterion the change in the frequency of bowel movements (increase or decrease), associated with abdominal pain.

questions.question: What is the main criterion for classifying abdominal pain as related to defecation in IBS?
options: Intensity greater than 7 on the EVA scale
questions.explanation: Abdominal pain related to defecation in IBS is defined by its temporal association with changes in bowel habit (frequency or consistency of bowel movements), according to the ROME IV criteria.

questions.question: What is the most accepted pathophysiological mechanism of abdominal pain related to defecation in IBS?
questions.explanation: Visceral hypersensitivity (abnormal perception of intestinal stimuli) together with alterations in intestinal motility are the central mechanisms in the genesis of pain related to defecation in IBS.

questions.question: Which subtype of IBS is most frequently associated with abdominal pain that improves after defecation?
questions.explanation: According to the ROME IV criteria, abdominal pain related to defecation can occur in all subtypes of IBS (diarrhea, constipation, mixed or unclassifiable), without exclusive predominance of one.

questions.question: What is the first-line pharmacological treatment for abdominal pain related to defecation in IBS?
questions.explanation: Tricyclic antidepressants (such as amitriptyline or imipramine) at low doses are the first-line treatment to modulate visceral hypersensitivity and reduce abdominal pain related to defecation in IBS.

questions.question: Which characteristic of abdominal pain suggests an organic rather than a functional origin in patients with IBS?
options: Night pain that wakes up the patient
Relationship with changes in depositional frequency
questions.explanation: Nocturnal pain that awakens the patient is an alarm feature that suggests an organic cause (such as inflammatory bowel disease), unlike the functional pain of IBS that typically does not disrupt sleep.

questions.question: Which of the following criteria must abdominal pain meet to be considered related to defecation according to ROME IV?
options: Associated with changes in stool frequency
questions.explanation: For the ROME IV criteria, abdominal pain related to defecation must be associated with changes in the frequency or consistency of bowel movements, forming part of the clinical picture of IBS.

questions.question: Which of the following stool patterns defines diarrhea in irritable bowel syndrome?
options: More than 3 bowel movements per day
A bowel movement every two days
Less than 3 bowel movements per week
questions.explanation: In irritable bowel syndrome, diarrhea is defined as more than 3 bowel movements per day, according to the Rome IV criteria, while the other options correspond to constipation or warning signs.

questions.question: Which subtype of irritable bowel syndrome is characterized by infrequent, hard stools?
questions.explanation: IBS with constipation (IBS-C) is defined by hard and infrequent stools, according to the Rome IV criteria, differentiated from the other subtypes by the evacuation pattern.

questions.question: When faced with a patient with IBS and a change in stool frequency, what finding requires ruling out another pathology?
questions.explanation: Occult blood in feces is a warning sign that requires ruling out organic diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease or neoplasia, unlike the typical functional symptoms of IBS.

questions.question: What first-line treatment is recommended for constipation-predominant IBS?
questions.explanation: Soluble fiber (psyllium) is the first-line treatment for IBS with constipation, as it improves the consistency and frequency of bowel movements, while loperamide is indicated for diarrhea.

questions.question: Which of the following Rome IV criteria is essential for diagnosing IBS?
options: Abdominal pain at least 1 day a week
questions.explanation: Recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day per week in the last 3 months, associated with a change in frequency or form of bowel movements, is the central Rome IV criterion for IBS.

questions.question: What change in stool frequency suggests a mixed pattern of IBS?
questions.explanation: Mixed IBS is characterized by the alternation of hard and liquid stools in the same patient, meeting criteria for both subtypes without one predominating over the other.

questions.question: What is the cardinal symptom of IBS that is relieved by defecation?
questions.explanation: Abdominal pain is the main symptom of irritable bowel syndrome and its relief with defecation is a key diagnostic criterion according to Rome IV.

questions.question: Which Rome IV diagnostic criterion links pain with defecation?
questions.explanation: The Rome IV criterion for IBS establishes that abdominal pain must be related to defecation, improving after evacuating, which differentiates it from other functional disorders.

questions.question: Which functional disorder shares defecatory relief but without pain?
questions.explanation: Functional constipation may present a feeling of relief after defecating, but it is not associated with recurrent abdominal pain, unlike IBS where the pain is central.

questions.question: How long must symptoms be present to diagnose IBS?
questions.explanation: According to the Rome IV criteria, IBS symptoms must be present for at least 3 months, with onset at least 6 months before diagnosis, to ensure chronicity.

questions.question: What change in defecation frequency is associated with IBS?
options: More than 3 bowel movements a day
Less than 3 bowel movements per week
questions.explanation: IBS can present with constipation, diarrhea or a mixed pattern; Alteration in defecation frequency is a supporting criterion, but not mandatory for the diagnosis.

questions.question: Which feature of abdominal pain suggests IBS?
options: Appearance only on an empty stomach
questions.explanation: IBS pain is typically related to defecation (appears or improves when evacuating), unlike organic pain that is usually constant or has other radiations.

questions.question: What is the main criterion for classifying IBS according to bowel habit?
questions.explanation: The classification of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) into subtypes is based on stool consistency assessed using the Bristol scale, not on the frequency, duration of pain or bloating.

questions.question: Which subtype of IBS is characterized by hard or goat-like stools in more than 25% of bowel movements?
questions.explanation: IBS with constipation (IBS-C) is defined by hard stools (Bristol type 1 or 2) in at least 25% of stools and soft stools in less than 25%.

questions.question: In which subtype of IBS do watery or pasty stools predominate in more than 25% of the stools?
questions.explanation: IBS with diarrhea (IBS-D) is characterized by soft or watery stools (Bristol type 6 or 7) in more than 25% of stools, with hard stools in less than 25%.

questions.question: What defines mixed IBS in the classification by intestinal habit?
questions.explanation: Mixed IBS (M-IBS) is defined by the presence of abnormal stools, both hard and soft, in more than 25% of the stools each, alternating over time.

questions.question: What tool is used to evaluate stool consistency in the classification of IBS?
questions.explanation: The Bristol Scale for Stool Shape is the standardized instrument that allows classifying fecal consistency into 7 types, essential for defining the subtypes of IBS.

questions.question: When is IBS classified as not classifiable according to bowel habit?
options: Does not meet criteria for other subtypes
questions.explanation: Unclassifiable IBS is applied when the consistency of the stool does not meet the criteria for IBS-C, IBS-D or IBS-M, that is, it does not reach the 25% threshold for hard or soft stools.

questions.question: Which of the following diagnostic criteria is used for constipation-predominant IBS?
questions.explanation: The Rome IV criteria are currently accepted for classifying IBS, including the constipation-predominant subtype, based on stool consistency.

questions.question: What type of stool predominates in IBS with constipation according to the Bristol scale?
questions.explanation: In IBS with constipation, the stools are hard and fragmented, corresponding to types 1 and 2 of the Bristol scale, which indicates slow colonic transit.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for constipation in IBS-C?
questions.explanation: Soluble fiber (such as psyllium) is the first line due to its bolus-forming effect and regulation of transit, unlike loperamide, which is used in diarrhea.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism is characteristic of IBS with constipation?
questions.explanation: Visceral hypersensitivity is a key mechanism in IBS, although transit is usually slowed; Increased secretion or excessive motility are not typical of constipation.

questions.question: Which guanylate cyclase C agonist drug is indicated for IBS-C?
questions.explanation: Linaclotide is a guanylate cyclase C agonist that increases intestinal secretion and accelerates transit, approved specifically for IBS with constipation.

questions.question: What test is recommended to rule out celiac disease in IBS-C before treatment?
questions.explanation: Anti-transglutaminase IgA antibodies are the recommended screening test for celiac disease, which can mimic IBS with constipation and should be ruled out.

questions.question: What is the main diagnostic criterion for diarrhea-predominant IBS (IBS-D)?
questions.explanation: IBS-D is defined by recurrent abdominal pain associated with soft or watery stools in at least 25% of bowel movements, according to Rome IV criteria.

questions.question: What first-line drug is recommended for the management of IBS-D?
questions.explanation: Loperamide is a first-line antidiarrheal in IBS-D, as it reduces stool frequency and improves stool consistency without significant prokinetic effects.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism is most associated with IBS-D?
questions.explanation: In IBS-D, visceral hypersensitivity predominates, which together with alterations in accelerated intestinal motility and abnormal secretion of water and electrolytes, generates diarrheal symptoms.

questions.question: What test should be ordered to rule out celiac disease in a patient with suspected IBS-D?
questions.explanation: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies are the serological test of choice to rule out celiac disease, which can mimic IBS-D and should be excluded before definitive diagnosis.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an exclusion criterion for IBS-D?
questions.explanation: Chronic abdominal pain is part of irritable bowel syndrome; The alarm criteria include occult blood, weight loss and fever, which require ruling out organic disease.

questions.question: What diet is initially recommended in the management of IBS-D?
questions.explanation: The low FODMAP diet (fermentable oligosaccharides, disaccharides, monosaccharides and polyols) reduces colonic fermentation and improves symptoms of bloating and diarrhea in IBS-D.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Involuntary weight loss is a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome, which requires ruling out organic diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease or neoplasia.

questions.question: What finding in stool is a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: The presence of blood in the stool is a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome, indicating possible lower digestive bleeding and the need for endoscopic study.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Crampy pain is a characteristic symptom of irritable bowel syndrome, but it is not considered a warning sign, unlike anemia, fever or weight loss.

questions.question: When should a warning sign be suspected in a patient with irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: The onset of symptoms after the age of 50 is a warning sign in irritable bowel syndrome, since it increases the probability of underlying organic pathology such as colorectal cancer.

questions.question: What test is recommended for a warning sign such as occult blood in stool in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: When faced with occult blood in feces, total colonoscopy is the test of choice to rule out colorectal neoplasia or inflammatory bowel disease in the context of irritable bowel syndrome.

questions.question: Which of the following findings requires reassessment of the diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Significant weight loss of 10% is a warning sign that requires reassessment of the diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome, as it suggests underlying organic disease.

questions.question: What is the main objective of requesting blood count in the study of irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Blood counts are requested to rule out iron deficiency anemia or other cytopenias, which may suggest organic diseases such as celiac disease or chronic blood loss.

questions.question: Which blood count finding suggests an organic cause rather than irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia, generally due to iron deficiency, is a finding that requires ruling out celiac disease, colorectal cancer or other organic causes, and is not typical of IBS.

questions.question: Why is TSH measured in the initial evaluation of irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: TSH is measured to rule out hyperthyroidism, which can cause diarrhea and IBS-like symptoms, and its treatment resolves gastrointestinal symptoms.

questions.question: In a patient with suspected irritable bowel syndrome, a suppressed TSH indicates:
questions.explanation: Suppressed TSH suggests subclinical or frank hyperthyroidism, which may manifest with diarrhea and abdominal pain, mimicking IBS; That is why it is included in the initial tests.

questions.question: Which of the following tests is considered part of the minimal diagnostic tests in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Blood count is a basic and minimum test recommended in the study of IBS to rule out anemia and other alterations that point to organic disease.

questions.question: A patient with IBS and elevated TSH should be evaluated for:
questions.explanation: Elevated TSH indicates hypothyroidism, which can cause constipation and other gastrointestinal symptoms; Its identification is relevant in the differential diagnosis of IBS.

questions.question: What is the most used antispasmodic in the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Pinaverium bromide is a selective antispasmodic of intestinal smooth muscle, indicated as a first line in irritable bowel syndrome to relieve abdominal pain and spasms.

questions.question: What type of fiber is recommended as a first line in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Soluble fiber (such as psyllium) is recommended initially because it improves stool consistency and reduces abdominal pain without exacerbating bloating, unlike insoluble fiber.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of antispasmodics used in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Antispasmodics such as pinaverium bromide act by blocking calcium channels in intestinal smooth muscle, reducing contractility and relieving visceral pain.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common adverse effect of antispasmodic use in irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Dry mouth is a common anticholinergic effect of some antispasmodics such as hyoscine, due to the inhibition of salivary secretion.

questions.question: What is the recommended starting dose of psyllium in the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome?
questions.explanation: The initial dose of psyllium is usually 3.4 g twice a day, adjusted according to the patient's response and tolerance, being effective in regulating intestinal transit without causing excessive distension.

questions.question: In which subtype of irritable bowel syndrome is the use of soluble fiber as the first line most indicated?
questions.explanation: Soluble fiber is especially indicated in IBS with constipation because it increases fecal volume and facilitates evacuation, improving the consistency of the stool.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of rifaximin in IBS without constipation?
questions.explanation: Rifaximin is a non-absorbable antibiotic that acts locally in the intestine, reducing bacterial overpopulation and improving IBS symptoms without constipation.

questions.question: In which subgroup of IBS is rifaximin specifically indicated?
questions.explanation: Rifaximin is approved for the treatment of diarrhea-predominant IBS (IBS-D), which corresponds to IBS without constipation, by improving bloating and stool frequency.

questions.question: What is the approved dosing schedule for rifaximin in IBS-D?
options: 550 mg three times a day for 14 days
200 mg twice a day for 10 days
1650 mg once a day for 5 days
questions.explanation: The approved regimen for rifaximin in IBS with diarrhea is 550 mg three times daily for 14 days, based on TARGET trials that demonstrated efficacy on global symptoms.

questions.question: What diagnostic criteria is used to classify IBS without constipation?
questions.explanation: IBS without constipation is classified according to the Rome IV criteria, which require recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day per week in the last 3 months, associated with changes in the frequency or form of bowel movements.

questions.question: What is the main adverse effect associated with rifaximin in IBS-D?
questions.explanation: Rifaximin has a favorable safety profile; The most common adverse effects are nausea and abdominal pain, with a low incidence of Clostridium difficile colitis due to its minimal absorption.

questions.question: In which patients with IBS-D is retreatment with rifaximin considered?
options: Recurrence of symptoms after initial response
Persistent diarrhea for more than 6 months
questions.explanation: Retreatment with rifaximin is indicated in patients who had a favorable initial response and present recurrence of symptoms, with studies that support up to three repeated cycles with sustained efficacy.

questions.question: What structure of the nervous system connects stress to intestinal function in IBS?
questions.explanation: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HHS) axis is the main neuroendocrine mediator of stress, and its activation alters intestinal motility and permeability in IBS.

questions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with visceral hypersensitivity in IBS due to stress?
questions.explanation: Serotonin (5-HT) regulates intestinal motility and sensitivity; its dysregulation due to chronic stress contributes to the visceral hypersensitivity characteristic of IBS.

questions.question: Which of the following factors most often exacerbates IBS symptoms?
questions.explanation: Psychosocial stress activates the HPA axis and alters intestinal motility, being the main trigger of exacerbations in patients with IBS.

questions.question: What type of treatment has proven effective in modulating the brain-gut axis in IBS?
questions.explanation: Cognitive-behavioral therapy reduces stress and modulates brain-gut communication, improving IBS symptoms by reducing visceral hypersensitivity.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism by which chronic stress alters the intestinal barrier in IBS?
questions.explanation: Corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) released by stress increases intestinal permeability by disrupting tight junctions, facilitating low-grade inflammation.

questions.question: What alteration of the brain-gut axis is typically seen in post-infectious IBS?
questions.explanation: After an infection, residual inflammation increases mast cells in the intestinal mucosa, which release mediators that sensitize visceral nerve fibers.
